text: Hello everyone, this is Dr. Madhya Leba. This is the second patient that was going to show up to my live connect surgery. Unfortunately, this patient cancelled. This is the subsequent video.

This patient has a symptomatic incisional ventral hernia with incarceration of bowel and omentum. She's had a prior C-section which is likely the cause of her problems. This can be quite difficult as the inferior portion of the lower abdominal area is very weak and the fascia in this area can be difficult to deal with. Most times there's actually no fascia, there's just muscle.

As per my routine, I like to go in triptomally, evaluate the hernia on this side, what type of repair I'm going to do. In this case, I wasn't sure if I was going to be able to do an ETEP or if I had to do a formal TAR or IPOM. Once I found what I was dealing with, I felt that I could do an ETEP. I first did a lateral dock on the left side, did a crossover, and then repositioned my ports for a top-down approach.
The surgeon performs a crossover dissection, staying high in relatively normal tissue, before completing a contralateral dissection and readjusting the ports for a top-down approach.

The surgeon begins a top-down approach by dissecting around the hernia site, confirming the absence of bowel within the hernia sac. The dissection reveals attenuated muscle and minimal fascia fibers, necessitating reconstruction of the posterior components and careful closure of the anterior components. The closure is successfully achieved despite the challenging tissue conditions.

The surgeon mobilizes the fragile peritoneum along the space of Retzius, performing bilateral lower abdominal transversus abdominis releases (TAR) to achieve closure and provide coverage for the suprapubic defect.
The surgeon anchors the sine core mesh to the pubis using several probing sutures, aiming to maintain mesh integrity and prevent displacement due to the large defect and unhealthy tissue.

I did mention at the live surgery from Connect that I typically don't use drains. However, I did place drains in this case. It's a game time decision usually. I'm a big fan of fibrin glue. This was placed in this case as well. And immediately after placement, I'll then insufflate the intra-abdominal region and this will then just collapse that space and hopefully seal it. In my mind I think it at least minimizes the risk of seroma. I mostly placed the drains in this case as it was quite loosey and I just felt I needed a couple drains for a couple days.

I really like going back into the abdomen and evaluating the repair. First off, there's no mesh interactions, no mesh exposure, no mesh potential complications. I don't see any bowel injury, no peritoneal tears, and I document all of this in the chart. While the glue is kind of settling in, and I have the drains or ports to suction, this collapses that space, and I'll do a laparoscopic tap walk with dexbarilla and marcaine, and these patients usually go home on the same day. Thanks for watching. This is the Second case that I was supposed to do the live surgery connect which canceled. So, thank you
